skill.name: building-logseq-plugins
skill.description: Guidance for creating Logseq plugins with the Plugin API, DB graph compatibility, Datalog queries, settings, UI commands, and development workflow.
skill.summary: Build Logseq plugins that work with Logseq DB graphs and the Plugin API.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and medium alerts, but review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential theft, or hidden execution logic. Most alerts are false positives from Markdown code fences, JavaScript template literals, ordinary description fields, and documentation links. The remaining risk is medium because the community skill grants Bash, Write, Edit, WebFetch, and WebSearch for plugin development workflows.

medium_findings.title: Broad Development Tool Permissions
medium_findings.description: The skill allows Write, Edit, Bash, WebFetch, and WebSearch, and it discusses npm development and build commands. This is legitimate for plugin creation, but users should review generated commands and file changes because community skills with these tools can affect local projects and access network resources.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tools and npm workflow are explicit in the skill file. The behavior is expected for plugin development, so the finding is elevated but not malicious.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external-command alerts correspond to Markdown code fences, inline code, JavaScript snippets, and template literals used as examples. No Ruby code, shell backtick execution, or hidden command execution was found in the skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation examples inside SKILL.md. Semantic review found instructional snippets rather than executable skill logic.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misclassified as Network Risk
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Logseq plugin documentation, the API reference, and sample plugins. These links support user research and do not exfiltrate data or call an unknown service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are public Logseq resources listed under a Resources section. No request code or credential transmission is present at these locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts map to ordinary words such as description text and a DB graph detection example. No MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata, user-facing descriptions, settings text, or graph type checks. They do not implement or recommend cryptographic algorithms.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Enabled Plugin Development Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash and references npm development and production build commands. This is expected for creating plugins, but command execution should remain visible and user-approved in marketplace use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The Bash permission and npm workflow are explicit. The risk depends on generated commands during use, not on malicious instructions in the skill file.

content.user_title: Build Logseq Plugins for DB Graphs
content.value_statement: Logseq plugin developers must handle new DB graph behavior and older markdown graphs correctly. This skill gives focused guidance for Plugin API usage, Datalog queries, settings, UI actions, and compatibility checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains the standard file structure for a Logseq plugin project.
Shows how to initialize plugins with @logseq/libs and logseq.ready.
Guides use of Editor, DB, App, UI, and Settings namespaces.
Compares DB graph and markdown graph query patterns.
Provides examples for slash commands, context menus, and settings schemas.
Lists development workflow steps for loading, testing, building, and publishing plugins.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not a complete plugin generator.
It does not verify current Logseq API changes beyond the documented examples.
It does not test plugins inside Logseq automatically.
It may require human review before running generated Bash commands.

use_cases.title: Create a New Logseq Plugin
use_cases.description: Plan the project structure, manifest, initialization flow, and local development workflow for a new Logseq plugin.

use_cases.title: Adapt Plugins for DB Graphs
use_cases.description: Review block properties, tag handling, Datalog queries, and feature detection for DB graph compatibility.

use_cases.title: Add User-Facing Plugin Controls
use_cases.description: Design slash commands, context menu actions, and settings that fit Logseq plugin conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a simple Logseq plugin. Include the required files, manifest fields, initialization flow, and local loading steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Slash Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Logseq slash command that inserts a reusable template. Explain the Plugin API calls and DB graph considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Update Query Logic for DB Graphs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Logseq plugin query approach for DB graphs. Explain what should change for properties, tags, pages, and blocks.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Plugin Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this Logseq plugin architecture for DB and markdown graph compatibility. Include API choices, settings, UI actions, and testing gaps.

output_examples.input: I want to build a plugin that inserts a reading template.
output: A short plugin structure with manifest requirements.
A slash command design for inserting the template.
Notes about structured properties for DB graphs.
Manual testing steps for loading the plugin in Logseq.

output_examples.input: My existing plugin queries page content and tags. What changes for DB graphs?
output: A migration checklist for title, tag, and property handling.
Guidance on using DB-oriented Datalog query patterns.
Compatibility notes for supporting both DB and markdown graphs.

best_practices: Test plugin behavior with both DB graphs and markdown graphs when supporting both formats.
Use feature detection before calling APIs that may differ between Logseq versions.
Keep generated commands and file edits visible during development.
anti_patterns: Assuming page names, block content, and tags have the same structure in every graph type.
Storing sensitive service credentials without warning users about plugin settings exposure.
Publishing a plugin without testing slash commands, context menus, settings, and build output.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete Logseq plugin automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, examples, and review support for plugin development.

faq.question: Does it cover the new Logseq DB architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It focuses on DB graph properties, tags, Datalog queries, and compatibility checks.

faq.question: Can it help migrate an older markdown graph plugin?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify query and property handling changes needed for DB graph support.

faq.question: Does it use external documentation links?
faq.answer: Yes. It references public Logseq SDK, API, and sample plugin resources.

faq.question: Is command execution required?
faq.answer: Some development workflows use npm commands. Review generated commands before running them.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is marked as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
